name: Insertion Sort
description:
Insertion sort keeps a sorted prefix on the left; each new element scans backward, shifting larger keys right until it slots in.

Best case O(n) when the input is already sorted. Average and worst case are O(n^2) because each insertion may walk the whole prefix. Stable and in-place with O(1) extra space—ideal for tiny arrays, nearly sorted windows, and as the base case in timsort-style hybrids. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Insertion_sort)
video: <iframe width="900" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/8oJS1BMKE64?list=PLZh3kxyHrVp_AcOanN_jpuQbcMVdXbqei" title="Insertion Sort" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

quiz.question: Which classification best fits this sorting algorithm?
options: Distribution-based (e.g. radix or bucket style binning)
Non-comparison-based (e.g. counting sort on bounded integers)

What is the worst case time complexity of Insertion Sort?
In which scenario is Insertion Sort most efficient?
options: When the array is large and unsorted
When the array is small and nearly sorted
When the array contains random elements
quiz.answer: When the array is small and nearly sorted

quiz.question: Does Insertion Sort work well with linked lists?
options: Yes, it works well with linked lists
No, it is not suitable for linked lists
It can work but not efficiently
quiz.answer: Yes, it works well with linked lists

name: Selection Sort
description:
Selection sort repeatedly chooses the smallest remaining element and swaps it into the next free slot of the sorted prefix.

Time is Theta(n^2) comparisons in every case, so unlike insertion sort you do not get an easy best-case speedup on sorted data. It uses O(1) extra memory but is unstable in the usual swap-based form. Mostly a teaching baseline. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Selection_sort)

Non-comparison-based (e.g. counting sort on bounded integers)

options: It is very complex to implement

What is the best case time complexity of Selection Sort?

name: Heap Sort
description:
Heap sort builds a binary heap, repeatedly extracts the extremum (max or min) to the end of the array, then heapifies the shrinking prefix.

Guaranteed O(n log n) time and O(1) auxiliary space—strong when you cannot afford merge sorts O(n) buffer. Not stable; constant factors are often higher than quicksort on random arrays. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heapsort)
video: <iframe width="900" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/_bkow6IykGM?list=PLZh3kxyHrVp_AcOanN_jpuQbcMVdXbqei" title="Heap Sort" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

name: Bubble Sort
description:
Bubble sort walks the array, swapping adjacent out-of-order pairs until a full pass makes no swaps.

Worst and average time O(n^2). With an early-exit flag, best case can be O(n) on already sorted input. Rare in production but useful to contrast with insertion sort in the Analyzer on nearly sorted vs random data. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Bubble_sort)
video: <iframe width="900" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/Cq7SMsQBEUw?list=PLZh3kxyHrVp_AcOanN_jpuQbcMVdXbqei" title="Bubble Sort" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

name: Quick Sort
description:
Quick sort partitions the array around a pivot so keys smaller than the pivot move left and larger keys move right, then recurses on both sides.

Average O(n log n) time with O(log n) stack depth when partitions are balanced; worst O(n^2) if pivots are always min or max (e.g. sorted input with naive pivot choice). Typically unstable; real libraries randomize pivots or use median-of-three. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Quicksort)
video: <iframe width="900" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/8hEyhs3OV1w?list=PLZh3kxyHrVp_AcOanN_jpuQbcMVdXbqei" title="Quick Sort (LR pointers)" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

quiz.answer: Partitioning the array around a pivot element

name: Merge Sort
description:
Merge sort splits the sequence in half, recursively sorts each half, then merges two sorted runs in linear time.

Stable, predictable O(n log n) time, but requires O(n) auxiliary space for the merge buffer—common trade-off when stability or worst-case guarantees matter. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Merge_sort)

code.python:
def mergeSort(arr):
    if len(arr) > 1:
        mid = len(arr)//2
        L = arr[:mid]
        R = arr[mid:]
        mergeSort(L)
        mergeSort(R)
        i = j = k = 0
        while i < len(L) and j < len(R):
            if L[i] < R[j]:
                arr[k] = L[i]
                i += 1
            else:
                arr[k] = R[j]
                j += 1
            k += 1
        while i < len(L):
            arr[k] = L[i]
            i += 1
            k += 1
        while j < len(R):
            arr[k] = R[j]
            j += 1
            k += 1
    return arr

name: Linked List
description:
A linked list stores elements in nodes chained by pointers instead of contiguous slots.

Insert or delete at a known node position is O(1); finding a value by key is O(n) without extra indexing. Arrays give better cache locality; lists avoid shifting and resizing costs. Doubly linked variants add a prev pointer for O(1) deletion between nodes. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Linked_list)

A data structure consisting of a sequence of elements in a fixed order.
A data structure used to store key-value pairs.
quiz.answer: A data structure consisting of nodes that contain data and a reference to the next node.

name: Binary Search Tree (BST)
description:
A BST is a binary tree where every node obeys the ordering invariant: left subtree keys are strictly smaller, right subtree keys are larger.

Search, insert, and delete follow one root-to-leaf path, costing O(h) for height h—O(log n) when the tree stays balanced, O(n) if keys arrive sorted without rebalancing. In-order traversal visits keys in sorted order. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Binary_search_tree)
video: <iframe width="900" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/ymGjUOiR8Jg?list=PLFonK3OU1E4qVvtRGBSL0xtXOmzZkLy8x" title="Binary Search Tree Animations | Data Structure | Visual How" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

quiz.question: Which invariant defines a binary search tree (BST)?
options: Each node has at most two children
For every node, left subtree keys are smaller and right subtree keys are larger
All leaves appear at the same depth
quiz.answer: For every node, left subtree keys are smaller and right subtree keys are larger

name: Stack
description:
A stack is a last-in, first-out (LIFO) collection: push adds on top, pop removes the most recent item.

Array-backed or linked implementations give amortized O(1) push and pop. Classic uses include DFS, undo stacks, and evaluating postfix expressions. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Stack_(abstract_data_type))

quiz.answer: A linear data structure that follows the LIFO (Last In, First Out) principle.

name: Queue
description:
A queue is first-in, first-out (FIFO): enqueue at the rear, dequeue from the front.

Ring buffers or linked structures keep both ends O(1). Breadth-first search, schedulers, and buffering pipelines map naturally to queues. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Queue_(abstract_data_type))

quiz.answer: A linear data structure that follows the FIFO (First In, First Out) principle.

name: Priority Queue
description:
A priority queue always exposes the highest-priority element next, independent of insertion order.

Binary heaps are the usual implementation: insert and delete-min/max in O(log n), peek in O(1). Heaps enforce parent-child order only—not a fully sorted sequence. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Priority_queue)

quiz.answer: A data structure where each element is associated with a priority.

name: Trie
description:
A trie (prefix tree) stores strings along edge-labeled paths from the root; shared prefixes share nodes.

Insert and exact lookup cost O(m) for string length m with alphabet-sized branching. Excellent for autocomplete and prefix counts; memory can grow with alphabet size and dense vocabularies. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Trie)

quiz.answer: A tree-like data structure used for storing a dynamic set of strings.

name: Heap
description:
A binary heap is a complete binary tree with heap order: in a max-heap each parent is greater or equal to its children (min-heap reverses the inequality).

Supports priority-queue operations in O(log n). Unlike a BST, a heap does not keep a full sorted order—only the extremum is cheap to read. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Heap_(data_structure))

quiz.answer: A special tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property.

quiz.answer: Heaps are complete binary trees, while BSTs are not necessarily complete.

name: Hash Table
description:
A hash table maps keys to buckets using a hash function, then resolves collisions when two keys land in the same slot.

Average-case lookup and insert are O(1) with a low load factor and a good hash; worst case degrades toward O(n) if everything collides. Chaining and open addressing are standard collision strategies. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Hash_table)

A special tree-based data structure that satisfies the heap property.
quiz.answer: A data structure that implements an associative array abstract data type.

Two different tables having the same data

quiz.answer: Two keys mapping to the same index

quiz.question: Which of the following is a common method to handle collisions in Hash Tables?

quiz.answer: The ratio of the number of elements to the size of the table

name: Dijkstra's Algorithm
description:
Dijkstras algorithm grows a set of nodes whose shortest distance from a source is known, relaxing edges in increasing distance order.

Works on graphs with nonnegative edge weights; typical binary-heap implementations run in O((V + E) log V). Negative edges break the greedy assumption—use Bellman–Ford instead. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Dijkstra's_algorithm)

Finding the minimum spanning tree of a graph.
quiz.answer: Finding the shortest paths between nodes in a graph.

Can Dijkstra's Algorithm handle negative weight edges?
options: Only if all edges have negative weights

What is a limitation of Dijkstra's Algorithm?
options: It does not work with weighted graphs.
It cannot handle graphs with negative weight edges.
It is not efficient for sparse graphs.
quiz.answer: It cannot handle graphs with negative weight edges.

name: Depth-First Search (DFS)
description:
DFS explores a graph by following an edge to an unvisited neighbor, recursing until no new moves remain, then backtracking.

Time O(V + E) with adjacency lists. Implementation uses recursion or an explicit stack. Good for connectivity, cycle detection, and topological sort on DAGs; for shortest hop count on unweighted graphs prefer BFS. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Depth-first_search)
video: <iframe width="900" height="500" src="https://www.youtube.com/embed/3_NMDJkmvLo?list=PLFonK3OU1E4qVvtRGBSL0xtXOmzZkLy8x" title="Depth First Search (DFS) Animations | Graph Traversal | Data Structure | Visual How" frameborder="0" allow="accelerometer; autoplay; clipboard-write; encrypted-media; gyroscope; picture-in-picture; web-share" referrerpolicy="strict-origin-when-cross-origin" allowfullscreen></iframe>

name: Breadth-First Search (BFS)
description:
BFS visits all nodes at distance k from the source before any at distance k + 1, using a FIFO queue.

Runs in O(V + E). Finds shortest paths in unweighted graphs (by edge count). Contrasts with DFS, which may dive deep before visiting nearby nodes. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Breadth-first_search)

name: Graph
description:
A graph is a set of vertices plus edges that may be directed or undirected, weighted or unweighted.

Adjacency lists yield O(V + E) traversals; adjacency matrices trade O(1) edge tests for O(V^2) memory on dense graphs. Pick algorithms based on constraints—BFS for unweighted shortest paths, Dijkstra for nonnegative weights, DFS for structure discovery. [Learn more](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/Graph_(abstract_data_type))
